briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 9, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis: Abnormal Phenomena Domestically and Internationally, and Ecological Emergency
sections.content: Domestically today, a phenomenon of children's persistent coughing has been reported [1], and voices have also emerged warning that Korea is not safe from heatwaves and droughts [3]. Internationally, unprecedented abnormal climate phenomena continue, such as record-breaking heatwaves in the continental U.S. in March [36, 70]. In particular, due to the rapid decrease in Antarctic sea ice, emperor penguins and Antarctic fur seals have been officially designated as endangered species [38, 40, 43, 47, 49, 54, 57, 64, 65, 66, 67, 68], and news of mass drowning of penguin chicks signals an ecological emergency [72]. Furthermore, with the possibility of the first climate change-linked marine fish extinction being raised and warnings that hidden ocean feedback loops could accelerate climate change [53, 39], the World Meteorological Organization has pointed out that energy imbalance leads to global warming and extreme weather events [60].

sections.title: ⚠️ Headwinds in Climate Policy and International Challenges
sections.content: In the international community, policy headwinds against climate change response are being detected. Climate change denial is resurfacing in Trump-era Washington [35, 37], and concerning movements have been observed, such as Yellowstone National Park deleting climate change-related pages from its key resource guide [55]. The European Union is reconsidering its climate change-related methane gas fine policy due to fuel supply concerns [62], and the war in Iran is drawing attention to the link between petrochemicals and climate change [63]. In France's 12 GW renewable energy auction, 'Europe First' policies have been strengthened, requiring a strategic shift from Korean companies [31]. News that Volkswagen has halted production of its flagship electric vehicles in the U.S. suggests the complex challenges of energy transition [74].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Efforts to Accelerate Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Amidst these crises, domestic and international efforts to achieve carbon neutrality and transition to renewable energy are actively continuing. Domestically, a roadmap for a carbon-neutral fuel ecosystem has been unveiled [4], and data-driven performance management has begun, including the verification of Goyang City's 1.07 million-ton greenhouse gas reduction [5]. Various entities continue to participate, such as Yangpyeong County's eco-friendly disposal of pruned fruit trees [6], Gwangju Shinsegae's spread of carbon neutrality practices [7], and Dangjin Bukbu Social Welfare Center's signing of an MOU [8]. Korea Southern Power has created a 'Carbon Neutral Forest' in Hadong County [9, 11, 13, 14]. The Korea Food Research Institute is accelerating the realization of carbon neutrality through technology that eliminates carbon dioxide from refrigerated transport vehicles [12], and information on the 2026 Carbon Neutrality Point Car Program has also been compiled, encouraging citizen participation [17, 19, 21]. In the renewable energy sector, a major energy transition centered on renewable energy is being promoted [25]. Gunpo City has signed an MOU to resource street tree wood waste into eco-friendly renewable energy [26, 28]. In the South Chungcheong Provincial Council, a resident-participatory renewable energy ordinance has passed the standing committee, seeking a community-based transition [27], and news has also been reported that investment sentiment for CS Wind in renewable energy has improved [30, 33]. Furthermore, TotalEnergies and Masdar are investing $2.2 billion in the Asian renewable energy sector, accelerating the transition to a low-carbon economy [34], and in Zimbabwe, a women-led renewable energy platform is leading Africa's energy revolution [29].

sections.title: 🌍 International Exploration for Climate Change Adaptation and Innovative Responses
sections.content: The search for adaptation and innovative response measures to climate change is also active internationally. Domestically, forums for discussion are being established, such as the Korea Environment Institute hosting a symposium on climate crisis response policies [2]. Internationally, research results have been presented suggesting that climate change can accelerate evolutionary rates [51], and there is also research indicating that ancient alleles in Alpine plant species help with climate change adaptation [44]. Efforts to strengthen climate resilience at the local level continue, such as farms in the Suffolk region receiving state funding to respond to climate change [52, 58]. Dutch homes are enhancing climate resilience with rainwater fences [73], and in Stratford, Ontario, Canada, a new river design challenge has begun due to climate change concerns [69], exploring creative governance and adaptation strategies. Furthermore, climate change scientists are competing in research to dim the sun [61], and the search for future-oriented solutions is also underway, with an emphasis on securing flexibility resources in AI and renewable energy transition [32]. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has released a meeting report on the advisory opinion of the International Court of Justice regarding climate change, emphasizing the need for international legal responses [50].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severity of the climate crisis, alongside the coexistence of various domestic and international efforts to overcome it. This is a time that requires continuous attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: US had hottest March on record as nation faced ‘unprecedented’ heat
articles.description: The continental US registered its most abnormally hot month in 132 years of records, according to Noaa data March’s persistent unseasonable heat was so intense that the continental United States registered its most abnormally hot month in 132 years of records, according to federal weather data. articles.title: Bitten by snakes 200 times – on purpose: US man’s quest to help deliver new antivenom
articles.description: Tim Friede put his ‘ass on the line’ to help stop snakebite deaths – whose numbers appear to be rising amid the climate crisisAs we overheat and degrade our planet, more people are likely to come into contact, sometimes fatally, with venomous snakes. One man hopes to provide an unusual solution to this, after subjecting himself to 200 intentional snakebites to his body.For nearly 20 years, Tim Friede, 58, allowed some of the most lethal snakes in the world to bite him so he could build up an imm

summary.phenomenon: A 58-year-old American man, Tim Friede, intentionally subjected himself to 200 snakebites over nearly 20 years.
summary.cause: He aimed to build immunity in his body to contribute to the development of new antivenoms. This initiative addresses the rising number of snakebite deaths amid the climate crisis, as the planet's overheating and degradation increase the likelihood of human contact with venomous snakes.

articles.original_title: Mass drowning of chicks puts emperor penguins at risk of extinction
articles.description: Record low levels of Antarctic sea ice are having grim consequences for penguins yet to grow waterproof feathersThe mass drowning of emperor penguin chicks as sea ice is melted by the climate crisis has led the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) to declare the species officially in danger of extinction.Emperor penguins rely on “fast” ice – sea ice that is firmly attached to the coast – for nine months of the year. It is where their fluffy chicks are hatched and grow until they

summary.phenomenon: Record low levels of Antarctic sea ice have led to the mass drowning of emperor penguin chicks that had not yet developed waterproof feathers. Consequently, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has officially declared the emperor penguin species endangered.
summary.cause: The melting of sea ice due to the climate crisis has resulted in the disappearance of 'fast ice'—sea ice firmly attached to the coast—which emperor penguins rely on for nine months. This fast ice is essential for the hatching and growth of their chicks.
summary.outlook: The emperor penguin species has officially become endangered.
